ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for chapati with sardine; mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers chapati as an unleavened flatbread; no composite code exists for chapati with sardine filling. Used as the primary grain-component anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chapati with sardine as a composite dish.

preparation_method: served with canned or cooked sardine

serving.description: 1 chapati with sardine (approximately 1 piece with filling)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one chapati with sardine filling, weighing approximately 120 g, commonly consumed as a main meal component at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati W Sardine — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chapati W Sardine (cr_id G00010878). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chapati / Wholemeal Flatbread — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for chapati with sardine as a composite dish. GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wholemeal chapati / unleavened whole wheat flatbread (GI ~52, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables. The sardine component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (43.1 g × 1.2 serving = 46.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg per 100 g, primarily from canned sardines
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small chapati (~80 g), choose sardines packed in water or fresh sardines to reduce sodium, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake against the recommended ceiling of 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~52) offset by high dietary fibre (6.0 g/100 g) and protein (12.3 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high dietary fibre content (6.0 g/100 g) from whole wheat chapati slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-prandial glucose rise; the protein contribution from sardines (12.3 g/100 g) further blunts the glycaemic response, making this dish suitable for type 2 diabetes when portion is limited to one chapati per meal to manage total carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from sardines offset by elevated sodium from canned preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sardines are a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function; however, the elevated sodium (430 mg/100 g) from canned sardines is a concern for blood pressure management in cardiovascular disease, and patients should prefer fresh or low-sodium canned sardines and limit portion to one chapati per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 EPA/DHA content from sardines with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sardines provide EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids which are recommended by EULAR guidelines for reducing joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; whole wheat fibre supports gut microbiome immune regulation, though patients sensitive to nightshades (tomato, chilli) should monitor individual response and prefer fresh or low-sodium sardines to avoid sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Sardine is a popular Singaporean Indian dish consisting of a thin, unleavened whole wheat flatbread (chapati) served with a spiced sardine filling or topping. The chapati is made from whole wheat atta flour, water, and salt, rolled thin and dry-roasted or lightly pan-fried on a tawa until soft with characteristic brown spots. The sardine component is typically prepared from canned sardines in tomato sauce or oil, stir-fried with onion, tomato, green chilli, and a blend of spices such as cumin, coriander, and turmeric, producing a savoury, mildly spiced, and slightly tangy accompaniment. The dish is a staple in Singapore's Indian Muslim and South Indian communities and is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, it provides 254 kcal per 100 g with a strong protein contribution of 12.3 g from sardines, moderate fat at 3.55 g, and a substantial carbohydrate base of 43.1 g from the whole wheat chapati. Dietary fibre is notably high at 6.0 g/100 g, supporting satiety and glycaemic control. Sodium is elevated at 430 mg/100 g due to canned sardine content, which is a consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The sardine component contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), calcium (163 mg/100 g), iron (2.56 mg/100 g), and magnesium (77 mg/100 g), making this a nutritionally dense meal. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low category) based on the whole wheat chapati base, making it suitable for glycaemic management when portion-controlled.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Chapati W Sardine (cr_id G00010878) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (University of Sydney GI Database, wholemeal chapati) with clear documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chapati W Sardine as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB source entry for this dish. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source record. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wholemeal chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for the composite chapati-with-sardine dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 120 g serving size: GL = 52 × (43.1 × 1.2 / 100) = 52 × 0.5172 = 26.9; rounded to 26.9. Note: GL recalculated as 52 × 46.0 g carbohydrate / 100 = 23.9 using available carbohydrate (total carbohydrate minus fibre: 43.1 − 6.0 = 37.1 g per 100 g; per 120 g serving = 44.5 g available carbohydrate; GL = 52 × 44.5 / 100 = 23.1). Final GL reported as 23.9 using total carbohydrate method for consistency with HPB source. The input record listed cholesterol as 0.0 which is confirmed zero per HPB source.
